question: [File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSupply(Bound timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted timestamp Bound where minTimestamp equals maxTimestamp to force accountBalanceRepository.getSupplyHistory() to return a single-point snapshot that misrepresents the true released supply of the Hashgraph ledger, causing downstream consumers to act on a falsified circulating supply figure?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSupply(Bound timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user send a timestamp Bound whose getAdjustedLowerRangeValue() returns a value just below Long.MAX_VALUE and adjustUpperBound() returns Long.MAX_VALUE, causing the getFirstDayOfMonth() arithmetic to overflow and silently shift the effective query window, returning supply data from an unintended historical period of the Hashgraph ledger?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSupply(Bound timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a Bound where the lower timestamp operator is GT with value Long.MAX_VALUE - 1, causing getAdjustedLowerRangeValue() to increment to Long.MAX_VALUE and bypass the minTimestamp > maxTimestamp guard via integer overflow, resulting in accountBalanceRepository.getSupplyHistory() being called with an inverted range that returns zero supply and a consensusTimestamp of 0, triggering EntityNotFoundException and masking the real Hashgraph supply state?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSupply(Bound timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user provide a timestamp Bound with a very large maxTimestamp (near Long.MAX_VALUE in nanoseconds) to getFirstDayOfMonth(), causing Instant.ofEpochSecond(0, timestamp) to throw a DateTimeException or silently wrap, making the optimalLowerBound calculation incorrect and causing the service to query a wrong historical window of Hashgraph account balances?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSupply(Bound timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Math.max(minTimestamp, optimalLowerBound) logic in getSupply() by supplying a minTimestamp that is smaller than the first-day-of-month offset, causing the effective query window to silently expand beyond the user-requested range and include additional historical Hashgraph balance snapshots that inflate or deflate the reported released supply?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSupply(Bound timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user send a Bound with adjustUpperBound() returning 0 (e.g., LT operator with value 1) and getAdjustedLowerRangeValue() returning 0, bypassing the minTimestamp > maxTimestamp guard (since 0 == 0), causing accountBalanceRepository.getSupplyHistory() to be called with lowerTimestamp=0 and upperTimestamp=0, returning the genesis-era Hashgraph supply state as if it were current?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getLatestNetworkStake()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call getLatestNetworkStake() to observe the consensusTimestamp field of NetworkStake and infer the exact timing of the most recent Hashgraph staking period update, enabling timing-based attacks that correlate with on-chain events to manipulate staking decisions based on leaked Hashgraph history metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getLatestNetworkStake()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getLatestNetworkStake() returns the full NetworkStake entity including stakingRewardRate, maxStakingRewardRatePerHbar, and stakeTotal to reconstruct the complete Hashgraph staking history by polling repeatedly over time, building a tampered historical record that misrepresents the network's staking evolution?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getLatestNetworkStake()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any rate limiting or authentication on getLatestNetworkStake() to flood the networkStakeRepository.findLatest() query, causing database contention that delays the ingestion of legitimate Hashgraph staking period updates and effectively freezing the mirror node's view of the current staking state?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user supply an arbitrary fileId value via NetworkNodeRequest to getAddressBookFileId(), causing the service to query an address book other than the canonical file 102, returning node topology data from a historical or non-canonical Hashgraph address book and misrepresenting the current network composition?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a NetworkNodeRequest with a fileId pointing to address book file 101 (the node address book) instead of 102, causing getNetworkNodes() to return a different historical view of the Hashgraph node topology that does not reflect the current consensus network, misleading clients about which nodes are active?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user send a NetworkNodeRequest with a very large nodeIds list (up to the effective limit) containing all possible node IDs, causing the nodeIdArray passed to networkNodeRepository.findNetworkNodes() to be extremely large and trigger a PostgreSQL query plan regression that bypasses index usage, causing a full table scan of address_book_entry and exposing all historical Hashgraph node records?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the combined EQ + range filter logic in getNetworkNodes() by supplying both EQ node IDs and a range that partially overlaps, causing the Range.closed(lowerBound, upperBound).contains() filter to silently exclude valid nodes, returning an incomplete view of the Hashgraph address book that misrepresents the active node set?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user send a NetworkNodeRequest with lowerBound=0 and upperBound=Long.MAX_VALUE (by omitting nodeId filters) to enumerate the complete Hashgraph address book history, including all historical node entries across all address book versions, leaking the full evolution of the Hashgraph network topology?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkNodeDto exposes adminKey bytes, nodeCertHash, and publicKey for every node in the Hashgraph address book, allowing reconstruction of the cryptographic identity of all historical consensus nodes and enabling targeted attacks against specific nodes based on their exposed key material?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a NetworkNodeRequest with multiple EQ nodeId parameters for the same node ID, causing the HashSet<Long> nodeIds deduplication to silently collapse them into one entry, and then combine with a range filter that excludes that node, returning an empty list that falsely implies the node does not exist in the Hashgraph address book?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user supply a NetworkNodeRequest with a GTE nodeId operator value of Long.MAX_VALUE, causing lowerBound to be set to Long.MAX_VALUE and upperBound to remain Long.MAX_VALUE, passing the lowerBound > upperBound check (since they are equal), and then querying networkNodeRepository.findNetworkNodes() with both bounds at Long.MAX_VALUE, potentially returning unexpected results from the Hashgraph address book?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the orderDirection string parameter passed directly to the native SQL CASE expression in findNetworkNodes() by observing that it comes from request.getOrder().name() (a Java enum), and verify whether any deserialization path allows injecting arbitrary strings into the orderDirection parameter that could alter the SQL query behavior and return Hashgraph node data in an unexpected order that bypasses pagination assumptions?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the serviceEndpointsJson field in NetworkNodeDto, which is constructed via a correlated subquery in findNetworkNodes() joining address_book_service_endpoint, to infer the IP addresses and ports of all historical Hashgraph consensus nodes, enabling targeted network-level attacks against the Hashgraph infrastructure?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the grpcProxyEndpointJson field returned in NetworkNodeDto to discover the gRPC proxy endpoints of all Hashgraph consensus nodes, and use this information to craft targeted denial-of-service attacks against specific nodes, effectively manipulating the Hashgraph consensus by taking nodes offline?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user send a NetworkNodeRequest with limit set to MAX_LIMIT (25) and a fileId pointing to an older address book, causing getNetworkNodes() to return historical Hashgraph node data that includes nodes no longer active in the network, misleading clients into routing transactions to defunct consensus nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getNetworkNodes(NetworkNodeRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the associatedRegisteredNodes field in NetworkNodeDto (which returns an empty array if null) to infer which Hashgraph consensus nodes have no associated registered nodes, identifying nodes that may be in a transitional state and targeting them for manipulation during the registration gap?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getRegisteredNodes(RegisteredNodesRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a RegisteredNodesRequest with a single EQ operator for registeredNodeId combined with additional range operators, triggering the IllegalArgumentException 'The eq operator cannot be combined with other operators' in resolveRegisteredNodeIdBounds(), and use the error message timing or content to enumerate valid registered node IDs in the Hashgraph network?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getRegisteredNodes(RegisteredNodesRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the deleted is false filter in RegisteredNodeRepository.findByRegisteredNodeIdBetweenAndDeletedIsFalseAndTypeIs() to confirm which registered nodes have been soft-deleted from the Hashgraph network by comparing results across different ID ranges, reconstructing the history of node deletions and inferring network topology changes?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/NetworkService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getRegisteredNodes(RegisteredNodesRequest request)] Can an unprivileged external user send a RegisteredNodesRequest with type=null to getRegisteredNodes(), causing nodeTypeId to be null and the SQL condition ':type is null or type @> array[:type]::smallint[]' to return all node types, exposing the complete registered node registry of the Hashgraph network including nodes of all types (e.g., MIRROR_NODE) that may contain sensitive topology information?
